elements.sent-2411-274: For a symmetric $n \times n$ matrix $M$ with eigenvalues $\{\lambda_i\}_{i = 1 ^n$ we let \begin{equation}G(M) := \sum_{i = 1}^n \tan^{-1}(\lambda_i).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-275: For $\lambda > 0$ to be fixed later let \begin{equation}\Phi(x) := \frac{\lambda x_1^2}{1+x_3} + \frac{\lambda x_2^2}{1-x_3}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-276: Each term in $\Phi$ is a translation of a one-homogenous function of two variables, so $D^2\Phi$ has rank two, and $\det D^2\Phi = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-277: We proved in \cite{MS that the Lagrangian angle \begin{equation}\Theta := G(D^2\Phi)\end{equation} satisfies \begin{equation}\label{ThetaExp} \Theta - \Theta(0) = 2\lambda(x_1^2 + x_2^2 + 2x_3^2) + O(\lambda^2)O(|x|^2).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-278: We choose $\lambda > 0$ small so that $\Theta$ is uniformly convex near $0$.
elements.sent-2411-279: Then for $\epsilon > 0$ small the connected component $\Omega_0$ of $\{\Theta < \Theta(0) + \epsilon^2\}$ containing $0$ is an analytic, uniformly convex set.
elements.sent-2411-280: We defined $w$ to be $\Phi$ in $\overline{\Omega_0}$, and for a small neighborhood $\Omega$ of $\overline{\Omega_0}$ we define $w$ in $\Omega \backslash \overline{\Omega_0}$ to be the solution to \begin{equation}G(D^2w) = \Theta(0) + \epsilon^2\end{equation} with the same first-order expansion as $\Phi$ on $\partial \Omega_0$ (obtained by Cauchy-Kovalevskaya).
elements.sent-2411-281: We proved that \begin{equation}\det D^2w < 0  outside of  \overline{\Omega_0}\end{equation} (so $w$ solves the degenerate Bellman equation \begin{equation}\max\{G(D^2w) - \Theta(0) - \epsilon^2,\, \det D^2w\} = 0),\end{equation} and combining this with the information that \begin{equation}\label{Hess} D^2w = 2\lambda(I - e_3 \otimes e_3) + O(\epsilon)\end{equation} in $\Omega$ we proved that $\nabla w$ is a diffeomorphism of $\Omega \backslash \overline{\Omega_0}$ (and similarly to above, maps $\Omega_0$ to a potato chip-like analytic surface compactly contained in $\nabla w(\Omega)$).
elements.sent-2411-282: We claim that provided $\lambda$, then $\epsilon$ were chosen small enough, we can find a function $\varphi \in C^{\infty _0(\Omega)$ such that, for all $t > 0$ small, $\nabla(w + t\varphi)$ is a diffeomorphism of $\Omega$ whose graph has smaller volume than that of $\nabla w$.}
elements.sent-2411-283: This confirms the non-minimality stated at the beginning of this section, after taking Legendre transforms.
elements.sent-2411-284: More precisely, we view the gradient graph of the Legendre transform $w^*$ of $w$ as being a rotation of the gradient graph of $w$, with a three-dimensional vertical portion (a rotation of the graph of $\nabla w$ over $\Omega_0$) over the surface $\nabla w(\Omega_0)$.
elements.sent-2411-285: We have for $t > 0$ small that $(w + t\varphi)^*$ is well-defined, and has $C^{1,\,1}$ gradient graph competing with that of $w^*$ but with smaller volume.
elements.sent-2411-286: To prove the claim we let $\rho \in C^{\infty _0(\Omega)$ be a cutoff function that is $1$ in a neighborhood $N$ of $\overline{\Omega_0}$, and we take \begin{equation}\varphi = (x_1^2+x_2^2-x_3^2)\rho.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-287: Using (\ref{Hess ) we have \begin{equation}\det D^2(w + t\varphi) = \det D^2w + (4\lambda^2\varphi_{33} + O(\epsilon)O(|D^2\varphi|))t + O(\lambda)O(|D^2\varphi|^2)t^2 + O(|D^2\varphi|^3)t^3.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-102: \begin{equation}\det D^2(w + t\varphi) = \det D^2w + (4\lambda^2\varphi_{33} + O(\epsilon)O(|D^2\varphi|))t + O(\lambda)O(|D^2\varphi|^2)t^2 + O(|D^2\varphi|^3)t^3.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-288: We conclude that \begin{equation}\det D^2(w + t\varphi) < 0\end{equation} for $t > 0$ small, using that $\varphi_{33 = -2$ in $N$, that $|\varphi_{ij | \leq 2$ in $N$, and that \begin{equation}\det D^2w \leq -\kappa < 0\end{equation} outside $N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-289: Similar arguments as in Lemma 2.4 of \cite{MS , using (\ref{Hess ), imply that $\nabla (w + t\varphi)$ is a diffeomorphism of $\Omega$ for $t > 0$ small.}}
elements.sent-2411-290: The first variation of the area of the graph of $\nabla w$ in direction $\nabla \varphi$ is given by \begin{equation}\int \sqrt{\det g}g^{ij}w_{kj}\varphi_{ki}.\end{equation}. Here \begin{equation}g = I + (D^2w)^2.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-291: Since the graph of $\nabla w$ is minimal outside $\Omega_0$, we just need to show that \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega_0} \partial_i(\sqrt{\det g}g^{ij}w_{kj})\varphi_k > 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-292: The expression $\partial_i(\sqrt{\det g g^{ij}\partial_j\nabla w)$ is $\sqrt{\det g}$ times the vertical component of the mean curvature vector to the graph $\Sigma$ of $\nabla w$.}
elements.sent-2411-293: The mean curvature vector of $\Sigma$ is given by the appealing formula \begin{equation}J\nabla_{\Sigma}\Theta,\end{equation} where \begin{equation}J(x,\,y) = (-y,\,x),\, x,\, y \in R^3\end{equation} and $\nabla_{\Sigma}$ is the gradient operator on $\Sigma$, as shown by Harvey-Lawson in \cite{HL1 .}
elements.sent-2411-294: Since the tangent plane to $\Sigma$ has slope $D^2w = O(\lambda)$, the expansion (\ref{ThetaExp ) gives \begin{equation}J\nabla_{\Sigma}\Theta = (0,\,0,\,0,\,4\lambda x_1,\,4\lambda x_2,\,8\lambda x_3) + O(\lambda^2)O(|x|).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-295: Since $\det g = 1 + O(\lambda^2)$ we conclude that \begin{equation}\label{MeanC} \partial_i(\sqrt{\det g}g^{ij}\partial_j\nabla w) = (4\lambda x_1,\,4\lambda x_2,\,8\lambda x_3) + O(\lambda^2)O(|x|).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-111: \begin{equation}\label{MeanC} \partial_i(\sqrt{\det g}g^{ij}\partial_j\nabla w) = (4\lambda x_1,\,4\lambda x_2,\,8\lambda x_3) + O(\lambda^2)O(|x|).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-296: Using (\ref{MeanC ), recalling the facts that $\varphi = x_1^2+x_2^2-x_3^2$ in $\Omega_0$ and \begin{equation}\Omega_0 = \{2\lambda(x_1^2 + x_2^2 + 2x_3^2) + O(\lambda^2)O(|x|^2) < \epsilon^2\},\end{equation} and making the change of variable \begin{equation}(x_1,\,x_2,\,x_3) = (2\lambda)^{-1/2}\epsilon(y_1,\,y_2,\,y_3/\sqrt{2}),\end{equation} we conclude that \begin{equation}\int_{\Omega_0} \partial_i(\sqrt{\det g}g^{ij}w_{kj})\varphi_k\,dx = 8\lambda \int_{\Omega_0} (x_1^2+x_2^2-2x_3^2 + O(\lambda)O(|x|^2))\,dx =\epsilon^5\lambda^{-3/2}\int_{\{|y|^2 + O(\lambda) < 1\}} (y_1^2+y_2^2-y_3^2 + O(\lambda))\,dy > 0,\end{equation} as desired.}
